original_abstract: Skeletal editing has emerged as a powerful synthetic paradigm for the precise atom-by-atom modification of core ring systems, offering a level of structural diversification that traditional peripheral functionalization cannot achieve. While recent advances in arene skeletal editing have leveraged carbene- or nitrene-type insertions and complex rearrangements, these methodologies have largely remained inapplicable to the pyridine nucleus. Here, we present a platform for the direct skeletal editing of pyridines via a formal CN-to-CC atom-pair swap, enabling the modular synthesis of substituted benzenes and naphthalenes. This transformation is achieved through a streamlined, one-pot sequence involving dearomatization, [4+2] cycloaddition, and rearomatizing retro-cycloaddition. By selecting specific cycloaddition partners, we can direct the installation of diverse substituents at precisely defined positions on the newly formed carbocyclic core. The utility and robustness of this approach are further underscored by the late-stage skeletal remodeling of several complex, pyridine-containing pharmaceuticals.
humanized_abstract: A myriad of complex natural products and biologically active molecules contain core aromatic ring systems that require precise modifications, namely individual atomic substitutions, to generate desired analogues for evaluation. Consequently, skeletal editing has proven to be a reliable and versatile method for such structural modifications. Although tremendous advances in the arene skeletal editing technology using either carbene or nitrene insertions accompanied by subsequent complex rearrangements have been demonstrated during the last decade, these approaches appear not to be applicable to pyridines. Here, we have demonstrated skeletal editing of pyridines using a formally CN-to-CC atom-pair exchange procedure by preparing, in situ, the reactive heterocyclic species by initial dearomatization of pyridines and the subsequent [4+2] cycloaddition between these reactive species and functionalized azulenes, which is then simply followed by rearomatization through a retro-cycloaddition to provide the desired hetero-and non-heterocyclic compounds with fully controlled substitution in a single-step (one-pot) reaction protocol. Notably, cycloaddition with modified azulene congeners allows for the unique introduction of substituents of choice in the final hetero on the condensed aromatic ring system. Late-stage skeletal editing has been successfully demonstrated using this technology for several existing FDA drugs that contain a pyridine ring as a critical element of activity.
